paragraphs.text: Rathenau Instituut Ref. Ares(2025)9073349 - 23/10/2025

evidence_quotes.quote: online tracking

paragraphs.text: Rathenau Instituut is an independent Dutch research institute contributing to socially responsible science, technology, and innovation (STI). For forty years, we have been stimulating public debate, organizing dialogue, and informing our Parliament, and Dutch and European policymakers on the impact of, for instance, social media, AI, immersive technology, neurotechnology, and online tracking on society. We therefore welcome the Commission's initiative to improve fairness for online consumers.

section_path: Response to the European Commission's public consultation on the Digital Fairness Act

evidence_quotes.quote: restricting targeted advertising.

paragraphs.text: In order to do so, there are two different policy directions the Commission can take: making changes within the current system of online tracking, or stimulating system change by restricting targeted advertising. We offer recommendations for both, but we would like to emphasize that the second option will lead to simplification of the legal framework, and provides the only way to effectively change the underlying incentive structure for companies, which causes most consumer and societal harms. First, we will demonstrate how these harms are caused, and question the net profitability of the online tracking model.

section_path: Response to the European Commission's public consultation on the Digital Fairness Act

evidence_quotes.quote: increasingly targeted and invasive personalisation of content such as social media timelines and advertisements.

paragraphs.text: Multiple studies from the Rathenau Instituut show that upcoming technologies such as Virtual Reality (VR), Augmented Reality (AR), neurotechnology, and generative AI, are incentivizing the collection of larger amounts of increasingly intimate data. Via smartphones, apps, browsers and games, but also through chatbots and wearables with an increasing amount of sensors, it is possible to track online behaviour and offline activities such as sleep, movement, emotional states and concentration. With (AI) analysing techniques that are becoming more advanced, all this data can be used for increasingly targeted and invasive personalisation of content such as social media timelines and advertisements.

evidence_quotes.quote: New platforms with new addictive design features are emerging.
generated more data which often is sold or used for targeted advertising.

paragraphs.text: New platforms with new addictive design features are emerging. For example, companion bots are often programmed to let confirmation take precedence over truth in their replies ("sycophant" behaviour). Another example: design choices such as moving dots while output to a query is being generated, make users attribute human traits to the chatbot ("anthropomorphization"). These design features tempt to keep users online, generating more data which often is sold or used for targeted advertising.

evidence_quotes.quote: advertisements and other content can be presented in a much more sensory, convincing and subliminal form.

paragraphs.text: Furthermore, new 'storefronts and billboards' are emerging: in VR-environments or through chatbot avatars, advertisements and other content can be presented in a much more sensory, convincing and subliminal form. All of the previous technological developments make it possible to intervene ever more deeply in the environments, experiences, and decisions of online consumers, whilst generating more data.

evidence_quotes.quote: datafication, personalisation and manipulation

paragraphs.text: Research from the Rathenau Instituut shows that this feedback loop of datafication, personalisation and manipulation, is putting increasing pressure on public values and human rights, such as privacy and autonomy, through unfair and problematic treatment of online consumers and internet users in general. These risks are exacerbated by the complexity and opacity of the online tracking ecosystem, which is dominated by Big Tech companies such as Meta and Google.

evidence_quotes.quote: harmful design features of online environments with centralised governance and ownership
the possibilities for malicious actors to uncover and misuse the identities of internet users are increasing

paragraphs.text: Our research shows how some harmful design features of online environments with centralised governance and ownership, jeopardize autonomy and inclusion. This is especially true for people in marginalised groups, such as LGBTIQ+-people, who face increased risk of being outed or having their content removed. Moreover, personal safety is at risk because the possibilities for malicious actors to uncover and misuse the identities of internet users are increasing, including intelligence officers and military personnel, human rights activists and people in vulnerable groups. National security is also a concern, for example because of the Chinese government possibly accessing the data of EU consumers for espionage and propaganda purposes, through apps such as Temu and TikTok.

evidence_quotes.quote: the increasingly harmful practices surrounding online tracking.

paragraphs.text: Although fundamental rights such as freedom of expression and privacy are protected by law, they lack enforcement online. Over the past fifteen years, existing and new EU laws have not been able to sufficiently tackle the increasingly harmful practices surrounding online tracking. Moreover, as established in the Commission's Fitness Check, these laws have formed a complicated and extensive legal framework, in which many overlapping and cross-referencing rules, with complex definitions, criteria and exceptions, have created many grey areas for companies.

evidence_quotes.quote: Virtually all of the concerns mentioned above can be traced back to the dominant revenue model on the internet: free services in exchange for personal data and exposure to targeted ads.

paragraphs.text: We therefore concur with the Commission's desire for simplification with the Digital Fairness initiative, the Digital Omnibus and the Digital Package of Simplification. But we believe the only way to simplify the legal framework without weakening it, is by drawing clear lines. Virtually all of the concerns mentioned above can be traced back to the dominant revenue model on the internet: free services in exchange for personal data and exposure to targeted ads. We therefore suggest reconsidering a ban on targeted advertising (as previously suggested by inter alia the European Parliament) to stimulate the transition to a different system, such as contextual advertising, paid services or
www.rathenau.nl/en

evidence_quotes.quote: online tracking and targeted advertising

paragraphs.text: donation models. If online tracking and targeted advertising remain allowed, the incentive for ever more personal data collection will remain intact, legal compliance with data protection and consumer law will remain an issue, and fundamental rights and Europe's democracy, strategic autonomy and security will remain under pressure.

evidence_quotes.quote: A share of the consumers finds personalization helpful

paragraphs.text: From the perspective of freedom of enterprise and the consumer's freedom of choice, there are arguments for allowing the current business model. A share of the consumers finds personalization helpful, website-owners can generate revue by selling ad space and data, advertisers not only reach consumers but can also gain insights into how users interact with advertisements, and especially smaller or local companies can target a certain audience without a large campaign.

section_path: The questionable profitability of online tracking

evidence_quotes.quote: Targeted advertising could therefore be more expensive than other forms of advertising.
The question is whether the higher price is passed on to the consumer.

paragraphs.text: However, it should be noted that even though there is a strong belief in the benefits of targeted advertising among companies, our analysis shows that the net welfare gain can be questioned. First of all, in the current global data market, a complex web of intermediaries (like databrokers, supply-side-platforms and demand-side-platforms) together take a margin estimated at 49%. Targeted advertising could therefore be more expensive than other forms of advertising. The question is whether the higher price is passed on to the consumer.

section_path: The questionable profitability of online tracking

evidence_quotes.quote: In addition, it is difficult to prove through empirical research that consumers buy something because they have seen a certain ad, or if they would have clicked on a certain ad or bought a certain product without personalization.

paragraphs.text: Moreover, it is not necessarily true that targeted advertisements lead consumers to the products with the lowest price and best quality: perhaps consumers would have benefited more from a comparison website, for example. In addition, it is difficult to prove through empirical research that consumers buy something because they have seen a certain ad, or if they would have clicked on a certain ad or bought a certain product without personalization. In other words, the effectiveness and cost efficiency of targeted advertising are difficult to demonstrate, especially for larger companies that already have high brand awareness.

section_path: The questionable profitability of online tracking

evidence_quotes.quote: data leaks, phishing, and other problems that go hand in hand with the online tracking system.

paragraphs.text: Furthermore, much profit flows to the big American companies behind browsers, search engines and social media platforms, who have limited competition, at the detriment of other (EU) parties. For example, a recent study by the Dutch Media Authority shows that Dutch media companies benefit very little from the growing digital advertising industry. Lastly, much (financial) damage is caused by data leaks, phishing, and other problems that go hand in hand with the online tracking system.

section_path: The questionable profitability of online tracking

paragraphs.text: www.rathenau.nl/en

section_path: The questionable profitability of online tracking

evidence_quotes.quote: personalized ads can offer specific advantages
targeting techniques can be used to exploit vulnerabilities and addictions, with negative consequences for the autonomy and well-being of consumers

paragraphs.text: All in all, personalized ads can offer specific advantages to parties (consumers, website and app owners, and advertisers), but it is uncertain whether the benefits outweigh the costs, and for which parties. Alternative business models might be able to offer the same advantages, without certain harms of online tracking. Because those harms are certain. Even though the net profitability of online tracking can be questioned, targeting techniques can be used to exploit vulnerabilities and addictions, with negative consequences for the autonomy and well-being of consumers, as stressed in the Commission's Fitness Check.

section_path: The questionable profitability of online tracking

paragraphs.text: Within the current system, updating, sharpening and clarifying rules could still improve online consumer protection to a certain degree. Options include:

evidence_quotes.quote: dark patterns

paragraphs.text: Clarify which dark patterns are in violation of the Unfair Commercial Practices Directive (UCPD), General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR) and Digital Services Act (DSA). For example: does a cookie banner with a green 'accept'-button and a see-through 'deny'-button allow for unambiguous consent? Does it constitute an illegal unfair practice when users need to click through to a different layer of the cookie banner to refuse consent? An alternative to clarification is explicit prohibition of certain dark patterns, for example by adding them to the blacklist of the UCPD.

evidence_quotes.quote: The European Commission recently ruled Meta's specific cross-tracking pay-or-okay model was not allowed under the DMA.

paragraphs.text: Clarify which pay-or-okay models are allowed or not, under the GDPR, UCPD and Digital Markets Act (DMA), so users do not have to pay for their privacy. The European Commission recently ruled Meta's specific cross-tracking pay-or-okay model was not allowed under the DMA. The question is whether this is allowed for other platforms or websites. When do users have a real alternative to a service? What constitutes a power imbalance?

evidence_quotes.quote: Clarify which data is prohibited to use for personal advertising, such as neurodata or eye tracking data.

paragraphs.text: Clarify which data is prohibited to use for personal advertising, such as neurodata or eye tracking data.

evidence_quotes.quote: special categories of personal data

paragraphs.text: Protect new categories of data as special categories of personal data, such as location data, or emotions and feelings.

evidence_quotes.quote: advertisements based on profiling using special categories of personal data are prohibited

paragraphs.text: Extend the strong protection offered by the new Transparency and targeting of political advertising act (TTPA) to all types of targeted advertisements. Meaning: advertisements based on profiling using special categories of personal data are prohibited, and advertisements based on normal personal data require explicit consent. Users who refuse consent must be given the option to use the service without advertisements. Publishers of advertisements need to adhere to extensive transparency requirements.

evidence_quotes.quote: pre-eminently addictive

paragraphs.text: Classify social AI as a high-risk category under the AI Act, to regulate these pre-eminently addictive, and big data-generating applications more strictly.

evidence_quotes.quote: Prohibit design choices that lead to sycophantic behaviour and anthropomorphism in chatbots.

paragraphs.text: Prohibit design choices that lead to sycophantic behaviour and anthropomorphism in chatbots. The AI Act requires chatbots to make it clear to users that they are dealing with AI, but this is unlikely to sufficiently protect consumers from all the harmful effects of the design choices.

evidence_quotes.quote: addictive design features
personalised recommender systems

paragraphs.text: Prohibit addictive design features as infinite scrolling and personalised recommender systems, as they are specially designed to maximize consumer engagement. Banning such practices could protect consumers from (unintentional) overconsumptions and manipulation of consumer behaviour.
www.rathenau.nl/en

evidence_quotes.quote: cross-tracking

paragraphs.text: The problem with these within-system improvements is that data collection and processing techniques are continuously developing at an accelerating speed; the legislator will keep playing catch-up. Take the constantly developing functionalities of Google Analytics for example, making it hard for supervisors to formulate guidelines that do not become obsolete quickly. Or Meta's pay-or-okay model: when the Commission ruled it in violation of the DMA because of cross-tracking, Meta had already begun updating its consent model to a more 'privacy friendly' one, that now needs to be evaluated against the law again.^[5]^

Furthermore, updating the legal framework will not be enough. Firstly, enforcement needs to be strengthened. National supervisors need more capacity and budget, innovation in the field of automating supervision needs to be stimulated, collaboration and coordination between supervisors in different member states needs to be improved (for instance through the proposed Digital Clearing House 2.0), companies need to be educated on the legal demands, and Big Tech companies need to be given higher fines, since it takes most of them less than two weeks to earn the amount of their yearly fines.^[6]^ However, due to the enormous and growing number of websites, apps and services, the enforcement burden is likely to continue to exceed the enforcement capacity.

evidence_quotes.quote: "consent fatigue" (the amount of misleading consent banners users encounter daily leading to habituation and irritation)

paragraphs.text: Secondly, this policy direction also requires addressing the information asymmetry between online consumers and companies. Tools as data commons and Personal Information Management Systems (PIMS) need further development and standardization, and place significant responsibility on often digital illiterate consumers. Furthermore, the potential effectiveness of public information campaigns instructing internet users on their rights and options to protect themselves can be questioned, partly due to the "consent fatigue" (the amount of misleading consent banners users encounter daily leading to habituation and irritation) and the "privacy paradox" (users indicating being concerned about their privacy, but not acting accordingly because they want free access to content and discounts, and are unaware of their rights). Even if consumers are technically 'informed' under GDPR standards, it is questionable whether individual consumers can be expected to understand the complexity of online tracking and data processing and make informed choices, when they are shown a list of 1000+ data-sharing parties, for example.

evidence_quotes.quote: the extensive, complex and opaque online tracking system
the consent model on which the legal framework is based unsuitable to sufficiently protect against its harms.

paragraphs.text: The foregoing policy direction constitutes a problem inherent to the extensive, complex and opaque online tracking system, making the consent model on which the legal framework is based unsuitable to sufficiently protect against its harms. As indicated
www.rathenau.nl/en

evidence_quotes.quote: online tracking is the driving force behind addictive and manipulative design

paragraphs.text: above, online tracking is the driving force behind addictive and manipulative design, hacking and other forms of misuse, and the dominance of some Big Tech companies. Therefore, in the interest of effectiveness and simplification, we recommend:

evidence_quotes.quote: Prohibit online tracking for the purpose of targeted advertising.

paragraphs.text: Prohibit online tracking for the purpose of targeted advertising. This approach would change the incentive structure and make companies switch to different advertising or revenue models.

While the relation between innovation and regulation is often assumed to be a purely negative one, historically, regulation has proven to be able to play a stimulating and guiding role in technological innovations. Consider the innovation-stimulating effect of strict emission standards for vehicles or the (implicit) ban on incandescent light bulbs. Regulation can also counteract controversial innovations, like with the ban on cloning.

evidence_quotes.quote: advertisements are matched to the content on platforms and webpages, rather than tailored to the individual user through tracking and profiling.

paragraphs.text: In a similar way, restricting online tracking can lead to innovation in contextual advertising, and achieve the necessary data minimization. In this advertising model advertisements are matched to the content on platforms and webpages, rather than tailored to the individual user through tracking and profiling. It has a somewhat outdated image, but it is making a comeback, and all kinds of innovative techniques are already being developed. Another system is paying for services instead of tracking (a "pay-or-nay model"). This would mean breaking with the narrative that has dominated the internet until now, namely that it is free and accessible to everyone – a narrative that might no longer be consistent with the marketplace the internet has already become – and paying for services and information just like in the offline world.
